Westmetall official cash for 29-Jun pending at run time — reference = SMM / Worthwill LME close.

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: ▼ ~2.4% (29-Jun LME close ~$3,099 vs 26-Jun official $3,176) — now ~20% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855 cash). Contango widened to ~-$14 as the front-end leads lower on easing near-term tightness.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 26-Jun official 306,725 t (Westmetall, latest; 29-Jun official pending). Draws continuing ~1,500 t/session, ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.3 days output — structural deficit intact through the selloff.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: 29-Jun LME close ~$3,085 cash (SMM / Worthwill; Westmetall official pending) vs 26-Jun official $3,164.00 — down ~$79 (-2.5%) as progress toward resuming Gulf exports and an easing Strait-of-Hormuz standoff unwound the remaining war premium. The 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855) is ~20% above. avg = May-2026 monthly mean ($3,670.18).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 29-Jun ~$3,099 (SMM / Worthwill LME close) vs 26-Jun official $3,176.00, -$77 (-2.4%). A firm dollar, rising China/Indonesia output and the Gulf-export resumption signal cap the tape; SHFE plunged on 30-Jun. avg = May-2026 mean ($3,600.79).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: 29-Jun cash ~$3,085 vs 3M ~$3,099 = -$14 = CONTANGO, deeper than -$12 on 26-Jun. The front-end is leading the decline as near-term scarcity fears unwind on the export-resumption signal; the structural deficit still skews the far curve.

benchmark.name: SHFE front-month (excl VAT)
benchmark.note: SHFE aluminium futures plunged on 30-Jun (SMM morning brief) as the Strait-management dispute and de-escalation pressured prices; East-China spot soft/at a discount. ~CNY 23,900/t SMM spot → USD excl-VAT equiv — verify SMM/metal.com.

benchmark.name: LME inventory (tonnes)
benchmark.note: 26-Jun official 306,725 t (Westmetall; 29-Jun official pending), ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.3 days output. Draws continuing through the selloff — the deficit is structural.

macro_events: SELLOFF RESUMED — DE-ESCALATION UNWINDS THE LAST OF THE WAR PREMIUM. The latest LME close (Mon 29-Jun) fell ~3% to ~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M as progress toward resuming Gulf exports and an easing Strait-of-Hormuz standoff removed the residual supply-risk premium; SHFE aluminium futures plunged again on 30-Jun. Price is now ~20% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855) and testing the lower end of the wave-C target zone.

Strait of Hormuz / Gulf exports: the de-escalation continues — a 60-day toll-free transit window holds, traffic is rebuilding, and the market is pricing a gradual resumption of Gulf aluminium exports. The remaining contested points (channel management, tolls) keep two-sided headline risk, but the directional read has flipped bearish for the premium.

Macro still caps the tape: the USD holds near a 13-month high (DXY ~101) with elevated Fed Sept-hike odds; Brent near a ~4-month low (~$73–74) as Gulf/Iranian barrels return. Rising China/Indonesia output into soft China demand reinforces the downside.

Floor intact but tested: Gulf capacity is still impaired (EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial) and LME stock keeps drawing (306,725 t, ~92% Russian-origin) — the structural deficit underpins the $3,000–$3,080 support shelf the market is now probing.
lme_commentary: Tue 30-Jun run — the latest LME close (Mon 29-Jun) fell ~3% to ~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M (SMM/Worthwill; Westmetall official pending), resuming the correction off the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855) as progress toward resuming Gulf exports and an easing Strait-of-Hormuz standoff unwound the residual war premium; SHFE futures plunged again on 30-Jun. The cash-3M curve deepened to a -$14 contango as the front-end leads lower. Macro still caps the tape — the USD near a 13-month high (DXY ~101), Brent near a ~4-month low (~$73–74), China/Indonesia output rising into soft China demand. The structural deficit is intact (EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial, LME stock drawing) and underpins the $3,000–$3,080 shelf now being tested. The move stays impulsively bearish unless ~$3,260 (.382) is reclaimed; a sustained break below $3,080 opens the $2,950/$2,894 (.618) zone.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$348/t FOB East Australia; Q2 oversupply pressuring

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region; easing on the building surplus

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$348/t) softer on Q2 oversupply vs Platts API region (~$306/t); Nalco guides $320–330/t for 2026 as Indonesia/India refinery capacity ramps into subdued demand — a building ~1 Mt+ surplus.
Gulf alumina-logistics risk continues to ease as Hormuz transit normalises — ~60% of regional alumina imports transit the Strait, and flows are rebuilding on the de-escalation.
Bauxite: Guinea's oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$34); Guinea weighing export curbs (a >$100/dmt target) — a structural upside risk to raw-material cost independent of the Gulf.
Refining: Gulf gas-supply risk minimal with the strait reopening; EGA's Al Taweelah refinery restart timeline (~12 months) remains the key supply swing factor.
Net: alumina balance skews to surplus (ample supply caps the price); de-escalation removes the logistics overlay, leaving Guinea bauxite-curb risk as the main floor under raw-material cost.
net_read: WAR PREMIUM FULLY UNWINDING — PRICE RESUMES LOWER ON GULF-EXPORT RESUMPTION SIGNAL. Aluminium's run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) was built on the Gulf supply shock (EGA Al Taweelah and ALBA outages, Strait-of-Hormuz disruption, ~3 Mt removed). The latest LME close (Mon 29-Jun) fell ~3% to ~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M as progress toward resuming Gulf exports and an easing Strait standoff removed the residual premium; SHFE plunged again on 30-Jun. The cash-3M curve deepened to a -$14 contango with the front-end leading. A firm dollar (DXY ~101, near a 13-month high), rising China/Indonesia output and soft China demand dominate the tape. The structural-deficit floor stays intact — EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial, LME stock drawing to 306,725 t (~92% Russian-origin) — and is now being tested at the $3,000–$3,080 shelf. US physical premiums stay tariff-inflated on the 50% Section 232 rate but are easing as the tariff component shrinks on a lower LME. The move stays impulsively bearish unless ~$3,260 (.382) is reclaimed; a break <$3,080 opens $2,950/$2,894.

feed.text: SHFE aluminium futures plunged again on 30-Jun (SMM) on the Strait-management dispute / de-escalation; East-China spot soft. The latest LME close (29-Jun) sits ~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M; Westmetall official for 29-Jun pending.
Aluminium fell ~3% (LME close ~$3,098.5, -3.11% per SMM/Worthwill) as progress toward resuming Gulf exports and an easing Strait-of-Hormuz standoff unwound the residual supply-risk premium.
Cash-3M curve deepened to a -$14 contango (from -$12 on 26-Jun) as the front-end led the decline — near-term scarcity fears unwinding on the export-resumption signal.
EGA expanded secondary-aluminium capacity to 400,000 t/yr with further growth planned in Europe and the US (SMM) — incremental recycled supply, separate from the impaired Gulf primary capacity.
Macro still a headwind: the USD holds near a 13-month high (DXY ~101) and Brent near a ~4-month low (~$73–74) as Gulf/Iranian barrels return.
SHFE aluminium and East-China spot at a discount to forward on weak downstream demand; rising domestic/Indonesian output keeps pressure on the ex-China complex.

Gulf capacity still impaired — EGA Al Taweelah ~12 months to restore, ALBA partial — and LME stock keeps drawing (306,725 t, ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.3 days output): the deficit floor underpins the $3,000–$3,080 shelf.

news.headline: Aluminium resumed its slide: the latest LME close (29-Jun) fell ~3% to ~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M (SMM/Worthwill); SHFE plunged again on 30-Jun. Westmetall official for 29-Jun pending.
news.source: SMM / Worthwill / Westmetall (LME, 29–30-Jun) — metal.com / worthwillaluminium.com / westmetall.com

news.headline: De-escalation continues: a 60-day toll-free Hormuz transit window holds and the market is pricing a gradual resumption of Gulf aluminium exports — unwinding the residual war premium. Contested channel-management/toll points keep two-sided headline risk.
China soft: SHFE aluminium and East-China spot at a discount to forward on weak downstream demand, while rising domestic/Indonesian output keeps pressure on the ex-China complex.
EGA expanded secondary-aluminium capacity to 400,000 t/yr, with further growth planned in Europe and the US — adding recycled supply even as its Gulf primary capacity stays impaired.

Section 232 aluminium tariff stays at 50% on full customs value (proclamation eff. 8-Jun-2026, through 2027) — US Midwest premiums remain structurally elevated (MW US Transaction ~$2,425/t) though easing on a lower LME; EU 6% duty + CBAM underpin European premiums.
MJP Q2 settled near an 11-yr high (~$351.5/t, +80% q/q); Q3 negotiations underway with de-escalation and high stocks skewing the settlement down toward ~$275–320 (Platts/Fastmarkets).
Gulf capacity still impaired (EGA Al Taweelah ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial) and LME stock keeps drawing to 306,725 t — the structural deficit provides a floor under the correction.

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián smelter restart completed Apr 2026. Q2 report due ~mid-Jul.

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due late Jul.

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 30-Jun (no new releases). Q2 season begins mid/late-July: Alcoa confirmed ~22-Jul (after close); Norsk Hydro late-Jul (has hedged a large share of 2026 primary output and premiums). Maaden (1211) reports Q2 in late-Jul/early-Aug. Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Alcircle, Discovery Alert, Investing.com.

commercial.text: The war premium is fully unwinding: the 29-Jun close fell ~3% to ~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M, curve in a -$14 contango. Protect realisation — keep LME hedges firmly in place on priced volume and accelerate pricing on unpriced tonnes rather than waiting on a catalyst-light recovery into a falling tape. Defend value-added (billet/slab/PFA) premium capture; avoid chasing the move down with discounts.

De-escalation keeps pressuring Asian premiums: CIF Japan spot is drifting (~$344) and Q3 MJP negotiations skew down toward ~$275–320 (from the spike-driven $350–353). Lock in remaining elevated premiums on renewals before the Q3 settlement resets lower, while EGA/ALBA capacity stays impaired. US MW remains structurally high (~$2,425/t) on the 50% Section 232 tariff but is easing as the tariff $ shrinks on a lower LME.

Ex-China FOB Australia (~$348/t) vs softer API region (~$306/t); the Q2 surplus caps it and Nalco guides $320–330 for 2026. If net seller, transact into any firmness promptly given the surplus bias; if buyer for smelting, the surplus offers softer windows — a favourable input-cost backdrop.

Normalise routing as Hormuz transit rebuilds and Gulf exports resume, but retain a measured contingency posture: keep war-risk cover and demurrage clauses live through the 60-day window in case of re-escalation, and hold the Jeddah/Red Sea land-bridge on standby until volumes fully recover.

Manage the turn with strategic accounts: the market has flipped from supply-scare to premium-unwind and price is resuming lower. Reaffirm reliability and OTIF, keep allocation transparent, and use dependability through the volatility to defend premium tolerance as buyers press for discounts.

Track: (1) whether $3,080 (.50) holds or the selloff opens $2,950/$2,894 (.618); a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) is needed to stabilise; (2) the pace of Gulf-export resumption and Hormuz transit counts / toll-channel disputes; (3) EGA/ALBA restart timelines (the remaining supply swing); (4) USD/Fed path; (5) China export pace (+12–18%), Indonesian ramp & Section 232 (50%); (6) Q3 MJP settlement (~$275–320) and Guinea bauxite-curb risk. Headline risk is two-sided: re-escalation upside vs a supply-led drift lower.

bottom_line: On 30-Jun the correction resumed. The latest LME close (Mon 29-Jun) fell ~3% to ~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M (SMM/Worthwill; Westmetall official pending) as progress toward resuming Gulf exports and an easing Strait-of-Hormuz standoff removed the residual war premium; SHFE plunged again on 30-Jun and the cash-3M curve deepened to a -$14 contango with the front-end leading. The structural deficit (EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial, ~92%-Russian LME stock drawing to 306,725 t) underpins the $3,000–$3,080 shelf now being tested, while the $4,000 upside scenario — contingent on a Strait shut >30 days — stays off the table. US physical premiums remain tariff-inflated under the 50% Section 232 rate but are easing on the lower LME. Net: the base case skews lower on a firm dollar, rising China/Indonesia output and the export-resumption signal; the move stays impulsively bearish unless ~$3,260 is reclaimed, with re-escalation the main upside risk.

caveats: Report run Tue 30-Jun-2026. The latest LME official on Westmetall remains the Fri 26-Jun close (cash $3,164.00 / 3M $3,176.00 / stock 306,725 t); the 29-Jun official had not posted at run time. 'Current' LME levels (~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M) are the 29-Jun daily close from SMM (metal.com) and Worthwill, which track the LME contract; figures should be reconciled against the Westmetall official once published.
LME stock is carried at the 26-Jun official (306,725 t, latest sourced) pending the 29-Jun official; the draw trend (~1,500 t/session) is reported but the specific 29-Jun figure is not yet public.

Live intraday CFD/FX feeds returned partly stale/cached snapshots at run time; macro/FX/energy values are latest available references (DXY ~101, Brent ~$73–74, gold ~$4,010) and should be re-validated at the London reopen.

Hormuz status: de-escalation continuing — a 60-day toll-free window holds, transit is rebuilding and Gulf-export resumption is progressing, but channel-management and toll points remain contested. Residual two-sided headline risk remains.

Daily physical premium assessments (MJP, CIF Japan, EDU, Rotterdam billets, US Midwest) sit behind PRA paywalls (S&P Global Platts / Fastmarkets); levels shown are the latest public/derived references and should be verified against the subscription assessments.

All figures are PUBLIC, sourced data. No confidential, internal or company-specific information is included; the only company-specific reference is public listed-company peer earnings (e.g., Maaden (1211)).

forward_path.basis: 29-Jun LME daily close (SMM/Worthwill; Westmetall official pending); -2.5% on the day as the residual war premium unwound on the Gulf-export resumption signal.
29-Jun LME close; curve deepened to a -$14 contango (cash vs 3M) with the front-end leading lower.
Analyst consensus; war premium removed, structural deficit retained. World Bank base ~$3,200.
World Bank — eases as Gulf supply recovers.

outlook.curve_note: Front-end contango deepened to -$14 as the 29-Jun selloff hit the cash leg hardest; the curve reflects easing acute near-term scarcity as Gulf exports look set to resume. Consensus 2026 average sits in the $3,200–3,500 band before easing into 2027 as Gulf supply is repaired. With the geopolitical catalyst fading, the curve is driven by the structural deficit (a floor) against rising China/Indonesia output and a firm dollar (the cap).

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

capacity_pipeline.timing: Structural ceiling on output; rising exports

capacity_pipeline.change: New smelter capacity ramping (BofA flags as added supply)

scenarios.drivers: Gulf re-escalation / Hormuz re-closure, EGA/ALBA restart slips, deficit deepens, China stays capped

Supply: Gulf restart slips — EGA Al Taweelah / ALBA recovery runs beyond ~12 months, or fresh outages
Inventory: visible LME stocks draw below ~300kt — days-of-cover critical
China: output stays capped at 45 Mt; net exports fall as domestic demand absorbs metal
Targets: Citi/Goldman ~$3,600, upside ~$4,000+ only if the Strait stays shut beyond ~30 days

scenarios.drivers: Ceasefire holds; structural deficit floors price against China/Indonesia supply — elevated but drifting lower
drivers_list: Geopolitics: ceasefire/Hormuz reopening holds — no corridor risk premium
Supply: structural deficit persists (~-365 kt 2026) with Gulf capacity repairing gradually (the floor)
China: exports strong (~+12–18% YoY); output near the 45 Mt cap — the bearish counterweight
Macro: USD firm after the hawkish June FOMC; soft China demand caps rebounds
Curve: contango holds; price oscillates $3,100–3,450 around the support band

ai_analysis: What changed: the correction resumed. After a tentative 26-Jun bounce ($3,164 cash / $3,176 3M), the 29-Jun close fell ~3% to ~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M as progress toward resuming Gulf exports and an easing Strait standoff unwound the residual premium; SHFE plunged again on 30-Jun.
Term structure: the cash–3M curve deepened to a -$14 contango (from -$12) as the front-end led lower; total LME stocks keep drawing (306,725 t) on the intact structural deficit.

Macro still dominant: the USD near a 13-month high (DXY ~101) with elevated Fed Sept-hike odds and Brent near a ~4-month low (~$73–74); dollar strength, rising China/Indonesia output and soft China demand reinforce the downside.

The remaining bull lever is supply, not corridor risk: EGA Al Taweelah (~12-month restore) and ALBA (partial) keep ~3 Mt impaired, underpinning a floor at the $3,000–$3,080 shelf. The $4,000 upside scenario — contingent on a Strait shut >30 days — stays removed by the de-escalation.

Forward bias: base case (50%) = elevated but drifting lower ($3,050–$3,400) as the ceasefire holds and the deficit floors price; bear 30% (durable de-escalation + China/Indonesia supply → $2,850–$3,080); bull 20% (Gulf re-escalation). Near-term watch: whether $3,080 (.50) holds or the selloff opens $2,950/$2,894, plus EGA/ALBA restart timing and the USD/Fed path.

premium_explainer.desc: Quarterly-negotiated premium over LME cash for primary metal delivered to Japan — the Asian benchmark.
Spot premium for cargoes delivered cost-insurance-freight to Japan; leads the next MJP negotiation.
Premium for non-EU-origin metal in-warehouse Rotterdam before the 3% import duty — the European spot gauge.
Value-added premium for extrusion billet (conversion + logistics) on top of the ingot premium.
Delivered-Midwest premium over LME; heavily driven by US Section 232 tariffs and import economics.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): MJP Q2 settled near an 11-yr high (~$351.5/t, +80% q/q); CIF Japan spot easing (~$344) with the LME selloff and de-escalation. Q3 negotiations underway with the export-resumption signal skewing the settlement down toward ~$275–320 (Platts/Fastmarkets).
European (EDU): ~$476/t, softer vs last month (~$519 avg) on weak demand but underpinned by the 6% duty + CBAM.
US Midwest: MW US Transaction ~$2,425/t; duty-paid P1020A ~$2,062/t — structurally elevated on the 50% Section 232 tariff but easing as the tariff $ shrinks on a falling LME.

Common thread: premiums spiked on the Gulf outage and are easing as the strait reopens and exports resume — unrepaired Gulf capacity keeps a floor while the policy (tariff/CBAM) overlay holds US/EU premiums structurally high.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$34); oversupply has roughly halved prices since Jan-25 — Guinea weighing export curbs toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Fastmarkets/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$348/t) easing on Q2 oversupply; API region softer (~$306; FOB futures ~$307). Surplus building into H2; the Hormuz reopening removes the Gulf-logistics risk.
Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$34) — Guinea's export-curb threat (a >$100/dmt target) is the key upside risk to raw-material cost.
Caustic soda firm (~$500/t FOB NE Asia) — a sticky refining cost; lime stable. Net refining-cost pressure eases with alumina/bauxite while caustic keeps a floor.
Read-through: softer alumina + bauxite lower a smelter's input cost; for an alumina seller, oversupply caps realizations into Q3 with the Gulf logistics risk now receding.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-traded — full transparency (3M ~$13,357/t, ~$6.06/lb, firm on strong China data; COMEX near records on US tariff premium); alloy input for 2xxx & some 6xxx

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Bulk-freight proxy for bauxite/alumina; ~2,550, near a multi-month low; Capesize BCI ~3,700 — Trading Economics / HandyBulk / Bloomberg

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.val: Reopening — transit rebuilding, Gulf exports resuming
logistics.note: ~80–85% of Gulf Al exports & ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz. Under the 17-Jun MoU a 60-day toll-free window is in force, transit is rebuilding and the market is pricing a gradual resumption of Gulf aluminium exports. Channel-management and toll points remain contested, so residual two-sided headline risk persists, but the directional read has turned bearish for the premium.

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: War-risk premia continuing to ease from the spike as transit normalises and exports resume; keep cover in place through the 60-day window in case of re-escalation.

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: Trucking workaround — keep on standby; less critical with the widened Hormuz route but retain as a live contingency while volumes stay below normal.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure and keep demurrage clauses live until routing fully normalises

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — watch PPAs/carbon), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, ~$13,640/t).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.
Logistics has flipped back to NORMAL: the Hormuz reopening reverses the war-risk premium — normalise routing, keep cover on standby; BDI near a 2026-cycle low (~2,550) eases bulk costs.

Procurement read: power + carbon (coke/pitch) drive roughly half of conversion cost — prioritise these for hedging / forward contracting; alloying metals are smaller line items but China-concentrated.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) topped at the $3,855 four-year high (early-Jun-2026) — a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave blow-off confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence — and an ABC correction is underway. Wave A bottomed at the 16-Jun $3,359 cycle low; wave B was a failed bounce that peaked at the 22-Jun $3,406 official (intraday spike to ~$3,607 on the contested reclosure) and was rejected at the .50. Wave C is extending: after a tentative 26-Jun bounce, the 29-Jun close fell ~3% to ~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M, pressing into the $3,000–$3,160 target confluence (the prior 4th-of-lesser-degree base / .382–.50 retrace) on the Gulf-export resumption signal. Base case (~55%): C completes near the .50 ($3,077)–.618 ($2,894) zone, but a confirmed turn needs a weekly close back above the .236 ($3,488). Bull alternate ~20% on a reclaim of $3,488/$3,666 reopening the highs; deep bear ~25% targets the .618 ($2,894) on a sustained break below $3,080. The bearish-primary count is invalidated only on a sustained weekly close above $3,488. Confidence: medium — structure, a deepening contango and a firm dollar lean bearish around the support shelf. Count carried unchanged from 29-Jun; no invalidation.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Short-term (daily): the decline off $3,855 unfolded as wave A into the 16-Jun $3,359 low; wave B bounced and spiked ~6% intraday to ~$3,607 on 22-Jun (the contested Hormuz reclosure) — the .50 retracement of the A-drop — and was rejected. Wave C extended below the wave-A low: after a brief 26-Jun bounce to $3,198, the 29-Jun close fell ~3% to ~$3,099 (-3.11%) into the $3,160–$3,000 target zone on the Gulf-export resumption signal, with SHFE plunging again on 30-Jun. Alternation held — a sharp 5-wave A produced a sharp, news-driven B. Base case: C is extending toward the $3,080 (.50) shelf and stays corrective/bearish unless $3,260 (.382) is reclaimed. Bullish re-trigger: a daily close above $3,488 (then $3,607) reopens a $3,666/$3,855 retest. Bearish-floor invalidation works the other way — a sustained break under $3,080 opens $2,950/$2,894. Confidence: medium — the rebound was sold and the tape is impulsive lower against a firm dollar. Count unchanged from 29-Jun.

premium_drivers.d: Gulf regional production remains curtailed (EGA/ALBA ~3 Mt) but the corridor risk is clearing: a 60-day toll-free window holds, Hormuz transit is rebuilding and the market is pricing a gradual resumption of Gulf aluminium exports — the war premium is unwinding, leaving a residual floor from the still-impaired capacity.
50% tariff on full customs value (eff. 8-Jun-2026, through 2027) → US Midwest premiums remain structurally elevated (~$0.20–0.21/lb market premium + tariff) even as the LME falls.
EU import duty reverted to 6% (from 4%, 1-Jan-2026) plus CBAM → European premiums structurally supported despite soft demand.
Gulf war-risk surcharges and freight easing as transit normalises and the closure declaration is repudiated; owners unwinding the spike in cover taken after 20-Jun.

so_what.line: Aluminium resumed its slide — the latest LME close (Mon 29-Jun) fell ~3% to ~$3,099/t 3M, now ~20% below the 02-Jun four-year high, as progress toward resuming Gulf exports and an easing Strait-of-Hormuz standoff unwound the remaining supply-risk premium; the move needs to hold above ~$3,080 to avoid opening the $2,950 zone.
points: Price turned back down: the 29-Jun LME close fell ~3% to ~$3,085 cash / $3,099 3M (SMM/Worthwill; Westmetall official pending) and SHFE plunged again on 30-Jun — the tentative late-week bounce has failed and the correction has resumed.

The driver is de-escalation: a 60-day toll-free Hormuz window holds, transit is rebuilding and Gulf aluminium exports look set to resume — unwinding the war premium. Contested channel/toll points keep some two-sided risk.

China is the drag: SHFE and East-China spot trade at a discount to forward on weak downstream demand, while rising Chinese and Indonesian output adds supply. The structural deficit (LME stock drawing to 306,725 t, ~92% Russian) provides the floor now being tested at $3,000–$3,080.

Watch this week: whether $3,080 (.50) holds or $2,950/$2,894 opens; a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) is needed to stabilise; Q3 MJP talks skewing lower (~$275–320); the dollar (DXY ~101); and any fresh Gulf headlines.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
